file_name: Bilyk et al. - 2025 - Integrating сonvolutional neural networks and autoencoders for skin lesion diagnosis.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning models, specifically an autoencoder for preliminary mole detection, a custom ResNet-50-based classifier for lesion classification (achieving an F1-score of 0.886), and a Mask R-CNN model for segmentation (achieving an IoU of 87%), on the HAM10000 dermatological dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development, training, and evaluation of deep learning models (autoencoder, ResNet-50, Mask R-CNN) for skin lesion diagnosis using the HAM10000 dataset. It reports specific performance metrics (F1-score of 0.886, IoU of 87%) and details the methodology, including data preprocessing and model configurations, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Integrating сonvolutional neural networks and autoencoders for skin lesion diagnosis
journal: MoMLeT-2025: 7th International Workshop on Modern Machine Learning Technologies
brief_summary: This study developed a deep learning pipeline for automated skin lesion diagnosis using the HAM10000 dataset, which includes images of seven dermatological classes. The approach integrates an autoencoder for preliminary mole detection, a custom ResNet-50 classifier for lesion classification, and a Mask R-CNN model for segmentation. Data preprocessing involved SMOTE balancing and augmentation, supplemented by 1,000 synthesized images to enhance variability. The ResNet-50 classifier achieved a validation F1-score of 0.886, outperforming other tested architectures like EfficientNet and VGG. Additionally, the Mask R-CNN model achieved an Intersection over Union (IoU) of 87% for accurate lesion segmentation. The findings suggest that combining these models creates a robust pipeline for clinical decision-support systems in dermatology.

lesion_types_studied.details: Non-invasive skin lesions included in the HAM10000 dataset used for classification experiments.
Common form of skin cancer included in the HAM10000 dataset used for classification experiments.
Pigmented lesions included in the HAM10000 dataset used for classification experiments.
Benign skin lesion included in the HAM10000 dataset used for classification experiments.
Benign tumors of melanocytes included in the HAM10000 dataset used for classification experiments.
Malignant tumor included in the HAM10000 dataset used for classification experiments.
Red or purple spots included in the HAM10000 dataset used for classification experiments.

all_task_types.target: Seven dermatological lesion classes (AKIEC, BCC, BKL, DF, NV, MEL, VASC) from the HAM10000 dataset.
all_task_types.details: A custom ResNet-50-based classifier was developed for lesion classification, achieving a validation F1-score of 0.886. The task involves predicting discrete diagnostic categories for skin lesions.

all_task_types.target: Precise masks delineating the boundaries of visible moles/lesions.
all_task_types.details: A Mask R-CNN model was employed for instance segmentation, achieving an Intersection over Union (IoU) of 87%. This task involves generating precise masks for each detected object.

all_task_types.target: Presence of a mole in an image (binary: mole present vs. absent/anomalous).
all_task_types.details: An autoencoder was used as a one-class classifier for preliminary mole detection/validation. It classifies images as containing a mole (PSNR > 25) or potentially anomalous/missing a mole based on reconstruction error.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dermatological dataset containing 10,000 dermoscopic images of moles with a resolution of 600x450 pixels in RGB format. It includes segmentation masks and covers seven classes of skin lesions.

use.training: Used for training the classification model (ResNet-50, EfficientNet, etc.) and the segmentation model (Mask R-CNN). The dataset was split into training (~83%), validation (~10%), and testing (~7%) subsets using stratified splitting. SMOTE was applied to balance minority classes.
use.validation: Used for validation of the classification model (~10% of the dataset).
use.testing: Used for testing the classification model (~7% of the dataset).
use.other: Used to generate 1,000 synthetic images for the segmentation model by placing mole fragments onto artificial backgrounds. Used for training the autoencoder for mole detection.

datasets_used.name: Synthetic Dataset
datasets_used.description: A set of 1,000 artificially generated images created by randomly placing 2–4 mole fragments from the HAM10000 dataset onto an artificial background, with object scaling and automatic segmentation mask generation.

use.training: Used to expand the dataset for training the Mask R-CNN segmentation model and the autoencoder for mole detection.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported; the paper relies on performance metrics (F1-score, IoU, PSNR) and model comparisons without statistical significance tests.

autoencoder_detection.notes: The autoencoder was used as a one-class classifier for preliminary mole detection. A PSNR threshold of 25 was established: values > 25 indicate a correct image (contains a mole), while values < 25 indicate an anomalous image. Config 2 achieved the highest PSNR.

Custom ResNet-50.notes: Achieved the highest performance among tested architectures.

classification.notes: Models were trained on the HAM10000 dataset with SMOTE balancing and stratified splitting (~83% train, ~10% validation, ~7% test). The custom ResNet-50 classifier achieved a validation F1-score of 0.886.

segmentation.notes: The Mask R-CNN model was trained on HAM10000 data augmented with 1,000 synthetic images. It achieved an IoU of 87% after 5 epochs.

Mask R-CNN.notes: Trained using 1,000 synthetic images generated by placing 2-4 mole fragments from HAM10000 onto artificial backgrounds.

segmentation_training.notes: Synthetic images were used to expand the dataset for the segmentation model and the autoencoder.

architecture_used.main_model: Integrated pipeline consisting of an Autoencoder (for detection), a Custom ResNet-50 (for classification), and Mask R-CNN (for segmentation).
architecture_used.pretraining: Mask R-CNN was trained in fine-tuning mode with most pre-trained network weights retained, except for the roi_heads module. Autoencoder and ResNet-50 pretraining details not explicitly reported.

autoencoder.type: Convolutional Autoencoder (adapted from Isuru Jayarathne and Michael Cohen [7])
autoencoder.structure: Convolutional layers used instead of fully connected layers for efficient processing of large images

segmentation.modifications: roi_heads module adapted to task specifics; rest of pre-trained weights retained

loss_functions.segmentation: Not explicitly stated (standard Mask R-CNN loss implied)

techniques: Data augmentation (30% probability of minor shifts, rotations up to 5°, scaling, brightness/contrast adjustments, normalization to [0,1])
training_details.environment: Python, PyTorch, scikit-learn, NumPy, Kaggle GPU environment

classification_or_output_head.autoencoder_output: PSNR (Peak-Signal-to-Noise Ratio) used as one-class classifier metric; threshold > 25 indicates mole present
classification_or_output_head.segmentation_head: Mask R-CNN roi_heads for instance segmentation masks

preprocessing_techniques: SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique) for class balancing
Data augmentation: light blurring (30% probability), minor shifts, rotations up to 5°, scaling, brightness and contrast adjustments
Pixel value normalization to the [0,1] range
Synthetic image generation: placing 2–4 mole fragments from HAM10000 onto artificial backgrounds with object scaling and automatic mask generation

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Minor shifts, rotations up to 5°, and scaling were applied.
Brightness and contrast adjustments were applied.
Used for class balancing to increase the number of samples for minority classes.

1,000 synthetic images were generated for the segmentation model by randomly placing 2–4 mole fragments from the HAM10000 dataset onto an artificial background with object scaling and automatic mask generation.

conclusions: A custom ResNet-50-based classifier achieved the highest performance among tested architectures, with a validation F1-score of 0.886, confirming its suitability for dermatological diagnostic tasks.
The Mask R-CNN model accurately detected and segmented all visible moles regardless of size or location, achieving an Intersection over Union (IoU) of 87%.

The integration of an autoencoder for preliminary mole detection, a ResNet-50 classifier for lesion classification, and a Mask R-CNN model for segmentation constitutes a robust pipeline for clinical decision-support systems.
The proposed pipeline provides valuable assistance for healthcare professionals in skin lesion analysis by addressing the limitation of traditional methods in handling multiple lesions.
The use of synthesized images with artificially added lesions enhanced dataset variability and realism, supporting the training of the segmentation and detection models.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is known to have significant class imbalance (e.g., NV class has 6705 images vs. DF class with 115) and potential selection bias from specific clinical centers; no external validation on independent datasets was performed.

The paper does not report statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values or confidence intervals) for the performance comparisons between models, relying solely on point estimates of metrics like F1-score and IoU.

Synthetic data augmentation was used for segmentation training by placing mole fragments onto artificial backgrounds; while this enhances variability, it may not fully capture the complexity of real-world clinical artifacts (e.g., hair, shadows, rulers) present in dermoscopic images.

The autoencoder for mole detection uses a PSNR threshold of 25 to distinguish between images with and without moles; this binary thresholding approach may lack robustness for borderline cases or images with high noise.

The classification model achieved a validation F1-score of 0.886, but the paper notes that some architectures (e.g., AlexNet) performed poorly (F1-score 0.105), suggesting high sensitivity to architectural choice and hyperparameter tuning.

The study is published in a workshop proceedings (MoMLeT-2025), which may indicate limited peer review depth compared to major journals; the authors also disclose using GPT-4 for grammar and style assistance.

The Mask R-CNN segmentation model is noted as 'computationally intensive' with training times of at least 40 minutes per epoch, which may pose deployment challenges for real-time clinical applications.

The paper acknowledges that traditional deep learning architectures (AlexNet, VGG, ResNet) are 'relatively outdated' compared to modern transformer-based approaches, yet it does not evaluate or compare against these state-of-the-art models.
